serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Black Sea charcoal linguine with prawns weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as listed on GrabFood.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (FDC ID 168936) and Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180); proportional composite estimate for charcoal linguine with prawns aglio olio
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for charcoal-infused linguine with prawns aglio olio. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries: cooked enriched pasta (~55% by weight), cooked shrimp/prawns (~25%), olive oil (~10%), garlic and chilli flakes (~10%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 185.71 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 8.57 g, carbohydrate 17.14 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. Activated charcoal contributes negligible macronutrients. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Pasta, spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~46–49)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for charcoal-infused linguine with prawns aglio olio. GI estimated at 48 based on the closest analogous food: white pasta (spaghetti/linguine), boiled, GI ~46–49 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The presence of protein (10 g/100g from prawns) and fat (8.6 g/100g from olive oil) further attenuates the glycaemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (48) × carbohydrate per serving (17.1 g/100g × 350 g / 100) / 100 = 48 × 59.85 / 100 ≈ 28.7; however, effective available carbohydrate after fibre deduction = (17.1 − 1.1) × 3.5 = 56 g; GL = 48 × 56 / 100 = 26.9, rounded to 14.4 per 100g equivalent for comparability. GL per serving (350g) = 48 × (17.1 × 3.5) / 100 = 48 × 0.5985 = 28.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 242.9 mg/100g; 850 mg per 350g serving approaches 37% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 200g or less, as a full 350g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, which represents a substantial fraction of the recommended daily maximum of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined pasta carbohydrate 17.1 g/100g; GI ~48 (low); protein and fat attenuate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~48) of linguine pasta combined with 10 g/100g protein from prawns and 8.6 g/100g fat from olive oil moderates postprandial glucose rise, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250g and monitor cumulative carbohydrate load, as a 350g plate delivers approximately 60g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil provides monounsaturated fat (~5.2 g/100g); saturated fat low at 1.4 g/100g; trans fat absent
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominance of monounsaturated fat from olive oil and the absence of trans fat make this dish broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, the sodium content of 242.9 mg/100g (850 mg per serving) warrants caution for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and portion control is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Olive oil oleocanthal and garlic provide anti-inflammatory compounds; chilli flakes are nightshade-derived
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olive oil's oleocanthal and garlic's organosulfur compounds offer anti-inflammatory benefit; individuals with RA who are sensitive to nightshades should reduce or omit chilli flakes, and consider supplementing with omega-3-rich fish such as salmon for stronger anti-inflammatory effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Sea is a Singaporean-Italian fusion pasta dish featuring linguine infused with activated charcoal, which gives the noodles a dramatic jet-black colour and a very mild, subtly earthy flavour. The pasta is boiled until al dente and then tossed in a classic Aglio Olio Peperoncino sauce — a simple but flavourful combination of extra-virgin olive oil, thinly sliced or minced garlic sautéed until golden, and dried chilli flakes that deliver a gentle heat. The dish is finished with succulent whole or halved prawns, which contribute a sweet, briny seafood flavour and a firm, juicy texture. The overall flavour profile is savoury, garlicky, mildly spicy, and lightly oceanic, with the olive oil coating each strand of pasta in a glossy, fragrant emulsion. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 185.7 kcal, 10.0g protein, 8.6g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), and 17.1g carbohydrate with a low glycaemic index of approximately 48. Sodium is borderline at 242.9 mg/100g, reaching approximately 850 mg per standard 350g plate. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for halal diets. It is commonly served as a main course at casual Italian-Singaporean restaurants and is available on food delivery platforms in Singapore. Clinically, the low GI, high monounsaturated fat content, and moderate protein make it a reasonable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk, provided sodium intake and portion size are monitored.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for charcoal-infused linguine with prawns aglio olio. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked enriched pasta and cooked shrimp, with olive oil and aromatics. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white pasta, boiled) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (cooked enriched pasta FDC 168936; cooked shrimp FDC 175180; olive oil FDC 171413) weighted by approximate ingredient proportions (pasta ~55%, prawns ~25%, olive oil ~10%, aromatics ~10%). The input record's per-100g values were broadly consistent and retained with minor rounding. 2. Activated charcoal contributes negligible macronutrients and is not separately listed in any approved nutrient database; its nutritional contribution is treated as zero. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single entry as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): olive oil and prawns contain no industrially produced trans fats; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for both ingredients. 5. GI estimated at 48 from analogous white pasta (boiled) per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); protein and fat content further attenuate glycaemic response. GL per 350g serving = 48 × (17.1 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 28.7. 6. Potassium estimated at 180 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central values for cooked shrimp (~259 mg/100g) and cooked pasta (~45 mg/100g) at stated proportions. 7. The dish is sold under the name 'Black Sea' on GrabFood Singapore; the canonical English name has been expanded to 'Black Sea Charcoal Linguine with Prawns Aglio Olio' for database clarity. 8. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (no pork, no alcohol in standard preparation); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 9. The food_group 'Fish and seafood' is retained from the input record as prawns are the primary protein source, though 'Mixed' or 'Grains and staples' could also be argued given the pasta base.
